design.title: DealFlow AI — Partnership Proposal for IT-Park Ventures
design.reference: Sequoia Capital / McKinsey pitch deck style

slides.title: Cover
slides.task_brief: Cover slide. Large bold headline: 'DealFlow AI'. Subtitle underneath: 'A Partnership Proposal for IT-Park Ventures'. Below that a thin line, then a one-liner tagline: 'AI-Powered Startup Screening — Transparent, Fast, Built for Your Deal Flow'. Bottom-left: small text 'Partnership Proposal 2025'. Use a clean white background with a subtle accent-blue geometric shape (like a large faint circle or angled stripe) in the top-right corner to add visual interest. The 'AI' in 'DealFlow AI' should be in accent blue color. No images on this slide. Speaker notes: Start by greeting the IT-Park team, say your name and that you're here to propose a technology partnership. Mention that you've been studying their fund's mission and built something specifically for them.

slides.title: The Problem
slides.task_brief: Headline: 'The Deal Flow Bottleneck'. Subtitle: 'Your analysts are spending most of their time on the wrong task.' Three large stat blocks in a row (bento grid style): Stat 1 — '80%' in large accent-blue number, label below: 'of analyst time spent on initial screening and basic research, not deep evaluation' (source: industry average across early-stage VCs). Stat 2 — '94%' in large red-500 number, label below: 'of startups that apply to accelerators are ultimately rejected, but analysts still review each one manually'. Stat 3 — '45 min' in large accent-blue number, label below: 'average time to research a single startup application — multiplied by hundreds of applicants per cycle'. Below the stats, a small italic line: 'Source: AEA Research on VC Selection (8,000+ deals analyzed); Affinity VC Survey 2024 — 85% of VCs now use AI tools for daily tasks'. Use image: https://sfile.chatglm.cn/images-ppt/fff3b9a362f5.jpeg as a subtle faded background element on the right side (low opacity overlay or placed in a card on the right). Speaker notes: Walk through each stat. Emphasize that IT-Park receives hundreds of applications per accelerator cycle. Analysts manually read every single one. The math is brutal — if you get 300 applications and each takes 45 minutes, that's 225 hours of work just for initial screening. That's almost 6 full weeks of one person's time, every single cycle.

slides.title: The Solution
slides.task_brief: Headline: 'DealFlow AI — Automated Initial Screening'. Subtitle: 'A transparent AI/ML engine that filters, evaluates, and ranks startups in seconds — so your analysts only spend time on the ones that matter.' Three feature cards in a horizontal row: Card 1 icon: 'upload_file', title: 'Batch Upload', body: 'Upload 1,000+ startup applications as a CSV. The system evaluates every single one in under 30 seconds.' Card 2 icon: 'psychology', title: 'Transparent AI Analysis', body: 'Every startup gets a confidence score with full explainability. No black box — you see exactly why each decision was made.' Card 3 icon: 'warning', title: 'Risk Detection', body: 'Automatically identifies red flags: single-founder risk, low funding velocity, market model concerns, and more — with written explanations.' Below the cards, a bold accent-blue banner: 'The AI does the initial filtration and research. Your analysts make the final investment decisions.' Speaker notes: Explain that DealFlow is not trying to replace analysts. It does the 80% of work that is repetitive and time-consuming — the initial filter, the basic research, the red flag check. The final 20% — the deep evaluation, the founder interviews, the gut instinct — that still belongs to the IT-Park team. The key word is transparent: every recommendation comes with a visible decision path.

slides.title: How It Works
slides.task_brief: Headline: 'How It Works — Three Steps from Application to Decision'. Show three steps horizontally with numbered circles (1, 2, 3) connected by arrows. Step 1: 'Input' — 'Startups enter via CSV upload (batch), manual form entry, or URL scraping. Works even for early-stage startups with no website — just 6-7 basic data points.' Step 2: 'AI Evaluation' — 'An LLM extracts structured data from applications. A trained Decision Tree ML model evaluates each startup based on: funding history, team composition, market type (B2B vs B2C), time-to-funding velocity, and founder track record.' Step 3: 'Ranked Results' — 'Every startup receives: a confidence score (0-100%), a list of strengths, a list of red flags with written explanations, and the exact decision tree path showing the logic. Export results as CSV.' Use image: https://sfile.chatglm.cn/images-ppt/8919295113ad.png as a small visual in the bottom-right area inside a rounded card with caption 'Conceptual UI'. Speaker notes: Walk through the three steps. Emphasize that Step 1 is designed for IT-Park's reality — many applicants are early-stage with no website, so the system accepts manual data entry and CSV uploads, not just URLs. Step 2 is where the magic happens — the ML model doesn't just guess, it follows a learned decision tree. Step 3 gives analysts everything they need in one view.

slides.title: The ML Engine
slides.task_brief: Headline: 'Not a Black Box — A Transparent Decision Engine'. Left half (60% width): Body text explaining the ML approach. 'DealFlow uses a Decision Tree classifier — the most interpretable ML model available. Unlike deep learning or neural networks, a Decision Tree shows you its exact logic path for every single decision.' Below that, show a styled text-based decision tree visualization (use a monospace/code block style with gray background): '|--- Has previous exit? --- YES --- Team size <= 12? --- YES --> INVEST (89%)' then '|--- Has previous exit? --- NO --- Funding > $4.5M? --- YES --- Time to first funding <= 18mo? --- YES --> INVEST (76%)' then '|--- Has previous exit? --- NO --- Funding > $4.5M? --- NO --- B2B? --- YES --- Rounds > 1.5? --- NO --> PASS (22%)'. Below the tree: 'This is a simplified example. The production model is trained on 5,000+ data points and evaluates 6 core features.' Right half (40% width): Use image https://sfile.chatglm.cn/images-ppt/4da4e071026d.png placed in a rounded card. Caption below: 'Decision Tree logic — fully auditable by any analyst'. Speaker notes: This is the slide that differentiates DealFlow from every other AI tool. Most AI is a black box — you put data in, you get a score out, and nobody knows why. A Decision Tree is different. You can literally read the logic. If IT-Park asks 'why did you reject startup #247?', the answer isn't 'the AI said so.' The answer is: 'It was rejected because the founder has no prior exit, total funding is under $4.5M, and it's a B2C company — and here is the data to prove each of those points.' That transparency is what builds trust.

slides.title: Before vs After
slides.task_brief: Headline: 'Before DealFlow vs After DealFlow'. Two-column comparison layout with a vertical divider. LEFT COLUMN (titled 'Current Process', with a subtle red-500 left border): Bullet list: 'Analyst opens each application manually', 'Spends 30-45 minutes researching basics (funding, market, team)', 'Writes notes on strengths and concerns', 'No standardized evaluation criteria — each analyst uses different judgment', 'After 2-3 weeks, the team narrows 300 applications down to 50 for interviews', 'Total time per cycle: 200+ analyst hours on screening alone'. RIGHT COLUMN (titled 'With DealFlow AI', with an accent-blue left border): Bullet list: 'Upload all 300 applications as a single CSV file', 'AI evaluates every startup in 30 seconds with consistent criteria', 'Each startup gets: confidence score, strengths, red flags, and decision logic', 'Analysts immediately see the top-ranked startups and the reasons why', 'First deep analysis meeting happens the next day, not next week', 'Total time per cycle: under 1 hour of analyst time on screening'. Bottom center: bold text 'That is a 95%+ reduction in screening time.' Speaker notes: This is the moment the audience does the mental math. Walk through the left column slowly — let them feel the pain. Then switch to the right column and emphasize the speed. The key number is 200+ hours down to under 1 hour. That is not a small improvement — it is a fundamental shift in how the fund operates. Ask them: 'What could your analysts do with 200 extra hours per cycle?'

slides.title: Why IT-Park
slides.task_brief: Headline: 'Why Build This With IT-Park Ventures?'. Subtitle: 'This is not a generic product — it is designed for your fund's specific reality.' Four bento grid cards (2x2 layout): Card 1 (top-left, icon: 'public'): 'Regional Focus' — 'IT-Park operates in Uzbekistan and Central Eurasia — a unique market with limited public data on startups. Generic tools like Crunchbase have minimal coverage here. DealFlow is designed to work with the data you actually have, not the data Silicon Valley tools assume you have.' Card 2 (top-right, icon: 'school'): 'Accelerator Model' — 'IT-Park runs an accelerator program, which means hundreds of applications per cycle, most from early-stage founders with minimal online presence. DealFlow accepts CSV uploads and manual entry because that is how your applications actually arrive.' Card 3 (bottom-left, icon: 'speed'): 'Lean Team, High Volume' — 'With a $10M fund and a focused team, every analyst hour matters. A tool that saves 200+ hours per cycle is not a luxury — it is a strategic advantage.' Card 4 (bottom-right, icon: 'lock'): 'Data-Driven Culture' — 'IT-Park already partners with global players like White Hill Capital. Building an AI evaluation engine positions the fund as the most technologically advanced VC in the region.' Speaker notes: Personalize this for IT-Park. Mention their $10M fund size. Mention that they already collaborate with White Hill Capital and Silicon Valley players. The point is: you are not pitching a generic SaaS tool. You are saying 'I studied your fund, I understand your constraints, and I built this specifically for your reality.' That level of customization is what turns a cold pitch into a real partnership conversation.

slides.title: Partnership Structure
slides.task_brief: Headline: 'The Partnership — A 30-Day Pilot Program'. Subtitle: 'Low risk for IT-Park, high value for both sides.' Left side (55% width): Two sections stacked vertically. Section 1 titled 'What We Need From IT-Park' (with accent-blue bullet points): 'Anonymized data on 50-100 past startup evaluations (features + outcome: success or fail). This is used to retrain the ML model on IT-Park's specific investment DNA.', 'Access for 1-2 analysts to test the tool on incoming deal flow during the pilot period.' Section 2 titled 'What IT-Park Gets' (with accent-blue bullet points): 'A custom-trained DealFlow instance fine-tuned on IT-Park's historical decisions.', 'Full transparency: every score, every red flag, every decision path is visible and auditable.', 'A dedicated development team that will iterate based on analyst feedback within 48 hours.', 'At the end of 30 days: a clear report showing time saved and evaluation accuracy.' Right side (45% width): A clean visual showing the pilot timeline — three phases stacked: Phase 1 'Week 1-2: Data Integration & Model Retraining', Phase 2 'Week 2-3: Analyst Testing & Feedback Loop', Phase 3 'Week 4: Results Review & Partnership Decision'. Use image https://sfile.chatglm.cn/images-ppt/0b0956857236.jpeg in a small rounded card at the bottom-right with caption 'Partnership in action'. Speaker notes: This is the ask. Be clear and specific. You are not asking for money. You are asking for data (anonymized, so there is no confidentiality concern) and a small amount of analyst time. In return, IT-Park gets a custom-built tool and a team that will iterate fast. The 30-day pilot structure reduces risk — if it does not work, they walk away. If it does, you move to a formal commercial partnership. End this slide by saying: 'We are not asking you to buy software. We are asking you to test a hypothesis with us.'

slides.title: Closing
slides.task_brief: Dark background slide (use slide-dark class — navy #0F172A background, white text). Large centered headline: 'Let us build this together.' Below it, a slightly smaller subtitle in gray-400 color: 'DealFlow AI is not just a tool — it is a partnership to make IT-Park Ventures the most data-driven fund in Central Asia.' Below that, a thin white divider line. Then three small action items in a row: '1. Review the live demo (we can set up a test with your data)', '2. Start the 30-day pilot this month', '3. Co-develop the production version together'. At the bottom: 'Ready to start? Let us talk.' in accent-blue color. No images. Speaker notes: Close with confidence. You have shown them the problem (real stats), the solution (transparent AI), how it works (three clear steps), the ML engine (visible decision tree logic), the impact (95% time reduction), why it fits IT-Park specifically, and a risk-free pilot structure. Now the ball is in their court. Do not over-talk here. Say: 'I built a working prototype specifically for IT-Park. I am not asking you to buy anything — I am asking for 30 days to prove it works with your data. If your analysts save even 10 hours a week, that is a win for both of us.' Then stop talking and wait for their response.
